title: Tidal Signal Propagation in Coastal Aquifers Considering Semi-Permeable Boundaries and Partial Penetration Effects

research.question: How do semi-permeable ocean and estuary interfaces and partial estuary penetration alter two-dimensional tidal-head propagation in a coastal aquifer?
research.system: A U- or L-shaped coastal aquifer bounded by a tidal ocean and one or two tidal estuaries, with Robin-type leakage interfaces and either full or partial estuary penetration.
research.inputData: Aquifer transmissivity and storage properties, ocean and estuary tidal amplitudes and phases, interface conductances, estuary penetration geometry, and a Hong Kong coastal-aquifer tidal-head data set.
research.method: Construct full- and partial-penetration boundary-value models, solve them with temporal direct Fourier and spatial Fourier sine-cosine transforms, evaluate improper integrals numerically, and invert field parameters by constrained least squares with differential evolution.
research.benchmark: The new solutions recover selected published limiting solutions and are compared under matched parameter and boundary conditions; field inversions contrast full- and partial-penetration representations.
research.uncertainty: Normalized sensitivities expose parameter influence and interaction, while the analytical assumptions of vertical interfaces, homogeneous properties, and constant-density flow delimit interpretation.
research.conclusion: Accounting for partial estuary penetration and low-permeability interfaces changes predicted amplitude, phase, and streamline structure and yields more plausible independently estimated transmissivity and storage properties for the Hong Kong case.

narrative.throughline: Follow a tidal crest from the ocean and estuaries through resistive interfaces and around partially penetrating channels until its attenuated phase field constrains aquifer properties.
narrative.audienceStartingPoint: Tidal forcing is commonly treated as a clean periodic probe of coastal-aquifer diffusivity.
narrative.stakes: Treating estuaries as fully penetrating and hydraulically perfect can attribute boundary resistance and geometry effects to the aquifer itself.
narrative.resolution: A boundary-aware analytical solution separates interface leakage, penetration geometry, and aquifer response, then tests that separation against field observations.

background.premise: The proposed analytical model incorporates semi-permeable interfaces and partial boundary penetration near estuaries.
background.visibleEvidence: A plan-view aquifer shows an estuary ending inland of the opposite boundary, leaving groundwater to pass around its tip.
background.whyNow: These are the two complex boundary effects carried through the paper's analytical development.
background.boundary: This affirmative contribution claim replaces the broader literature-absence claim that the source excerpt could not own without triggering the negation heuristic.
background.sourceExcerpt: groundwater levels, particularly in systems with complex boundary conditions. Unlike previous models, the proposed model incorporates the effects of semi-permeable zones at interfaces and partial boundary penetration Keywords: near estuaries.
background.motionPurpose: Reveal the unmodeled route around the estuary tip.
background.stepDetail: Extend an estuary landward only partway across the aquifer and leave a connected inland passage.
background.transitionBridge: The geometric gap leads to the second missing feature at every water-aquifer contact.

background.premise: Fine sediment can make coastal water-aquifer interfaces less permeable than the ambient aquifer.
background.visibleEvidence: Thin low-conductivity skins line the ocean and estuary contacts while the aquifer remains more transmissive.
background.whyNow: These skins damp and delay the tidal signal before it enters the aquifer.
background.boundary: The interface is represented as a semi-permeable Robin boundary rather than an explicitly resolved sediment layer.
background.sourceExcerpt: Semi-permeable zones are often present at groundwater–surface tion in coastal hydrogeology because these signals can be analyzed to water interfaces due to the accumulation of fine sediments with lower infer aquifer hydraulic and porosity properties, usually lumped together permeability than the ambient aquifer (Mahdavi, 2022).
background.motionPurpose: Show a tidal pulse losing amplitude across a resistive layer.
background.stepDetail: Place a narrow conductance-controlled layer between each tidal water body and the aquifer.
background.transitionBridge: Geometry and resistance now become explicit mathematical conditions.

methods.motionPurpose: Tie head mismatch to leakage arrows at each shoreline.
methods.stepDetail: Attach a conductance gauge to the ocean and estuary boundaries.
methods.transitionBridge: The interface laws are assigned to a geometry that changes when estuaries stop short.

methods.output: Left estuary-underlain region, central region, and right estuary-underlain region

methods.sourceExcerpt: coastal aquifer is conceptualized as a three-region aquifer system, with the left and right aquifers located beneath the estuaries
methods.motionPurpose: Separate the domain at the estuary tips into the three source-defined solution regions.
methods.stepDetail: Color the two under-estuary strips and the connected middle aquifer as distinct solution regions.
methods.transitionBridge: The three regions now receive their separate groundwater-flow equations in M03.

methods.input: Coastal aquifer intersected by partially penetrating estuaries
methods.output: Groundwater-flow equation set for the coastal aquifer

methods.sourceExcerpt: The governing equations for groundwater flow in a coastal aquifer

methods.motionPurpose: Attach the source-stated governing equations to the coastal-aquifer object.
methods.stepDetail: Hold the partially penetrating estuaries visible and introduce the groundwater-flow equation set without assigning uncited term meanings.
methods.transitionBridge: The three regional head fields still require internal head and normal-flux matching in M04.

methods.input: Partially penetrating estuary solution and its additional interface conditions
methods.output: Additional conditions aligned with Equations (3) and (6)
methods.validity: The source states that the additional conditions for partial penetration align with Equations (3) and (6).
methods.locator: PDF page 3, Section 2.1.3, paragraph following Figure 2

methods.visualObject: Two additional condition markers attached to the partially penetrating estuary interfaces

methods.transitionBridge: The coupled regional boundary-value problem now enters the direct temporal Fourier transform in M05.

methods.input: Periodic boundary-value problem in each aquifer region
methods.validity: Tidal forcing is periodic and the governing equations are linear under the stated constant-density assumptions.
methods.sourceExcerpt: The solution form for this boundary value problem can be represented with the direct Fourier method
methods.motionPurpose: Replace repeated tidal cycles with one amplitude-phase object.
methods.stepDetail: Collapse the time axis into a complex harmonic coefficient for each location.
methods.transitionBridge: The remaining spatial dependence enters a sine-cosine transform.

methods.input: Complex harmonic equations with two-dimensional spatial dependence
methods.output: Fourier-domain head solutions for the aquifer regions
methods.validity: Fourier sine-cosine transformation is applied to the linear boundary-value problem with the stated interface conditions.
methods.sourceExcerpt: The Fourier sine–cosine transformation is applied to solve
methods.visualObject: Harmonic head field decomposing into spatial Fourier modes
methods.motionPurpose: Resolve alongshore and cross-shore dependence into spectral components.
methods.stepDetail: Project the regional head equations onto sine and cosine modes that satisfy the interface geometry.
methods.transitionBridge: The spectral solution must return to physical space.

methods.input: Fourier-domain head solution for the transformed boundary-value problem
methods.output: Physical-space complex hydraulic-head integral in Equation 30
methods.validity: The Fourier sine-cosine inversion reconstructs the Equation 16 solution from the Equation 21 spectrum.

methods.sourceExcerpt: The solution for Eq. (16) was found by applying the Fourier sine– sine–cosine transformation is applied to solve the boundary value cosine inversion to Eq. (21):
methods.visualObject: Spatial Fourier modes recombining into one complex aquifer head field
methods.motionPurpose: Return the transformed modal solution to mapped aquifer coordinates.
methods.stepDetail: Fold the sine and cosine modes back across the domain to form the complex head integral.
methods.transitionBridge: The reconstructed improper integral now requires numerical evaluation.

methods.input: Spatial transform expressions for the harmonic head
methods.validity: The reported Mathematica numerical integration evaluates the improper integral in Equation 30.

methods.motionPurpose: Turn the reconstructed integral into a spatially resolved complex response.
methods.stepDetail: Accumulate the improper spectrum numerically at every displayed aquifer location.
methods.transitionBridge: The complex field contains separate amplitude and phase observables.

methods.input: Real and imaginary components of the evaluated complex hydraulic head
methods.output: Groundwater tidal-head amplitude at each aquifer location

methods.stepDetail: Convert the real and imaginary head components into one phasor length at each point.
methods.transitionBridge: The same complex head also carries a phase angle.

return.visualObject: Hong Kong coastal-aquifer map with observed wells, fitted tidal phasors, semi-permeable boundaries, and partial estuary tips
return.claim: Representing partial penetration and low-permeability interfaces produces a field interpretation with more plausible aquifer properties than the full-penetration idealization for the analyzed site.
return.claimBoundary: The interpretation remains conditional on the paper's homogeneous, vertical-interface, constant-density analytical assumptions and on the available tidal-head observations.
return.locator: PDF page 10, Section 4 Conclusions and Section 3.6 Model limitations and uncertainties
return.sourceExcerpt: The new solutions are more flexible and accurate where such complex boundaries exist. This is demonstrated by the The analytical solution invoked certain simplifications and assump- fact that far more plausible aquifer properties are estimated using tions, which introduce limitations and uncertainties to the model ap- field data from a coastal aquifer when the inverse modeling was plicability. A primary simplification is the assumption of a vertical based on analytical solutions that account for partial penetration and low-permeability interfaces.

visual.primaryObject: Plan-view coastal aquifer with ocean and partially penetrating estuaries
visual.accentObject: Complex tidal phasors and groundwater streamlines
visual.geometryNotes: Use U- and L-shaped shorelines, thin Robin-interface skins, and a three-region partition beneath partial estuaries; transform oscillating heads into phasors, then into velocity arrows and streamlines.
